claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 16 and in approved education; Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin determining your Child Benefit eligibility.

**First, I need to ask about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I actually have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex who's 16, and Blake who's 5 years old. Should I go through them one at a time, or can we discuss both?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're very welcome, and that's a great question. We can absolutely discuss both Alex and Blake together - it will be more efficient that way!

Let me work through the eligibility for each child. Since they're in different age categories, some questions will apply to one but not the other.

**Here's what I know so far:**
- **Alex** is 16 years old (falls into the 16-19 age category)
- **Blake** is 5 years old (falls into the under 16 category)

Let's start with **Alex (age 16)**:

For children aged 16-19, eligibility depends on their education or training status. Is Alex currently:
- In **full-time non-advanced education** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar)? OR
- On an **approved training course** (such as a government-funded training programme)?

And for **Blake (age 5)**:

For children under 16, I just need to confirm one thing: Is Blake working **full-time** (more than 24 hours per week in paid employment)?

Please let me know the answers for both children, and we'll continue from there!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Right, so for Alex - yes, he's still in college doing his A-levels.

And Blake? No, he's only 5, he's in primary school, not working at all!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! That's really helpful.

**Good news so far:**
- **Alex (16)** doing A-levels qualifies as full-time non-advanced education ✓
- **Blake (5)** in primary school and not working - that's exactly what we'd expect! ✓

Now I need to check a few more things that apply to both children:

1. **Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?** (Such as Employment and Support Allowance, Universal Credit, or Income Support claimed by them personally - not benefits you receive for them)

2. **Is Alex on a paid apprenticeship?** (A-levels wouldn't be, but I need to confirm he's not doing an apprenticeship alongside his studies)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, neither of them get any benefits in their own names - they're just kids! 

And no, Alex isn't doing an apprenticeship, just his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Both children are looking good so far. Now I need to ask some questions about **you** (the person who would be claiming the benefit).

**Residency Check:**

Do you normally live in the UK? 

Or if not, do any of these situations apply to you?
- You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
- You have **pre-settled status** and are working, seeking work, studying, or have sufficient resources
- You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

For most people, simply living in the UK is enough to satisfy this requirement. Can you confirm your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement! ✓

**Responsibility Check:**

Now I need to confirm you're responsible for Alex and Blake. You're considered responsible if **any** of these apply:

- You **live with** the children, OR
- You **contribute to their upkeep** (paying for food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.) at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount, OR
- You pay **maintenance** for them under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Blake, or are you contributing to their care in another way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake lives with me at home. But Alex doesn't live with me - he lives with his mum. I do help out with Alex though - I contribute money towards his upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So to summarise:

- **Blake** lives with you ✓ - that clearly meets the responsibility requirement
- **Alex** lives with his mum, but you contribute to his upkeep

For Alex, since you contribute financially towards his care, I need to check: is your contribution at least equivalent to the Child Benefit amount (or more)? This could include money for food, clothes, pocket money, housing costs, presents, etc. - whether paid weekly, monthly, or as lump sums.

Also, an important question: **Is Alex's mum (or anyone else) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This matters because only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any one time. If someone else is already claiming for Alex, we'd need to consider the rules around that.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, I contribute money for Alex - I help with things like clothes and school expenses. I can give you the exact amount if you need it?

And yes, his mum already claims Child Benefit for him, and Alex lives with her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for being so clear about this - that's really helpful information.

**For Alex**, this is an important situation: since Alex's mum already claims Child Benefit for him **and** he lives with her, she has priority for the claim. The rules state that the person the child lives with generally takes precedence.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This confirms what I thought. Let me explain the situation:

**For Alex:**
Since his mum already claims Child Benefit for him and Alex lives with her, you would **not** be able to claim Child Benefit for Alex unless:
- His mum agrees to transfer the claim to you, OR
- There's a dispute, in which case HMRC would decide (and they typically prioritise the person the child lives with)

**For Blake:**
Blake lives with you, so let's continue checking his eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake**, I just need to confirm a couple more things:

Is Blake currently:
- In **hospital** for an extended stay?
- In **local authority care**?
- In a **foster care** arrangement?
- Or is he living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is just living normally at home with me - nothing unusual, just regular home life.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, almost there!

**Final question for Blake:**

Is the local council paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance? (For example, if he were in council-supported housing or receiving council financial support for his care.)

For most children living at home in a regular family situation, the answer would be **no** - the council isn't paying.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - he just lives at home with me like any normal kid.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

Here's the outcome of the assessment for both children:

---

### ✅ Blake (age 5) — **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake. He meets all the requirements:
- He's under 16 ✓
- He's not working ✓
- He doesn't receive benefits in his own right ✓
- You live in the UK ✓
- He lives with you ✓
- The council isn't paying for his care ✓

---

### ❌ Alex (age 16) — **NOT ELIGIBLE (for you to claim)**

Although Alex would normally qualify (he's 16 and doing A-levels, which is approved education), **you cannot claim Child Benefit for him** because:
- Alex lives with his mum, not with you
- His mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him
- Only one person can claim at a time, and priority goes to the person the child lives with

**Note:** This doesn't mean Alex isn't covered — his mum's claim ensures he's receiving Child Benefit. It just means you personally can't make a separate claim for him.

---

## Next Steps for Blake

To claim Child Benefit for Blake, you should:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Blake
3. **Provide Blake's birth certificate**
4. Payments are made **every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receive certain benefits)

⚠️ **Important:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, be aware of the **High Income Child Benefit Charge** — you can still claim, but some or all may need to be repaid through tax.

Do you have any questions about the results or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is 16 and in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), which would normally qualify. However, Alex lives with his mum, not with you, and his mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Under the rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and priority is given to the person the child lives with. Since Alex lives with his mum and she already claims for him, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex unless she agrees to transfer the claim to you.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), which automatically qualifies for the child age requirement. Blake is not working (as expected for a 5-year-old in primary school). Blake does not receive any benefits in his own right. You live in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. Blake lives with you, so you meet the responsibility requirement. Blake is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered). The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with them and meets all criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim for Alex because Alex lives with his mother who already claims Child Benefit for him, and priority goes to the person the child lives with.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 16 and in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), which would normally qualify. However, Alex lives with his mum, not with you, and his mum is already claiming Child Benefit for him. Under the rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time, and priority is given to the person the child lives with. Since Alex lives with his mum and she already claims for him, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex unless she agrees to transfer the claim to you.

Blake is 5 years old (under 16), which automatically qualifies for the child age requirement. Blake is not working (as expected for a 5-year-old in primary school). Blake does not receive any benefits in his own right. You live in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements. Blake lives with you, so you meet the responsibility requirement. Blake is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostered). The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, who lives with them and meets all criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim for Alex because Alex lives with his mother who already claims Child Benefit for him, and priority goes to the person the child lives with.
